whatIfScenarios.title: What if the knee was hot, red, and severely swollen?
whatIfScenarios.description: Acute monoarticular arthritis with fever, cannot bear weight

chiefComplaint.original: My knee has been bothering me for months
chiefComplaint.modified: My knee blew up overnight - it's hot, red, and I can't walk on it. I have a fever.

feedback.correct: Hot, swollen joint with fever = SEPTIC ARTHRITIS until proven otherwise. MUST tap the joint (arthrocentesis). WBC >50,000 with >90% PMNs highly suggestive. Emergent orthopedic consult for washout. IV antibiotics after cultures.
feedback.partial: Good concern for septic joint. What's the critical next step?
feedback.incorrect: Acute hot swollen joint = SEPTIC ARTHRITIS until proven otherwise. JOINT TAP IS MANDATORY. Send for cell count, Gram stain, crystals, culture. Septic joint is orthopedic emergency - needs washout. IV antibiotics after cultures. Don't mistake for gout initially.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: OA = chronic, no systemic symptoms, normal temp. Septic arthritis = acute, fever, hot joint, can't bear weight. Gout = can look similar, but crystals on tap and usually afebrile or low-grade. When in doubt, tap it.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the knee was locking and giving way?
whatIfScenarios.description: Knee catches and locks in position, mechanical symptoms after twisting injury

chiefComplaint.original: My knee has been bothering me for months
chiefComplaint.modified: My knee catches and locks when I walk. It happened after I twisted it playing tennis. Sometimes it gives out completely.

feedback.correct: Locking + giving way + twisting injury = meniscal tear likely. McMurray and Thessaly tests. MRI confirms. Many heal conservatively with PT. Surgery (partial meniscectomy or repair) for persistent mechanical symptoms or large tears.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of meniscal pathology. What exam findings support this?
feedback.incorrect: Mechanical symptoms (locking, giving way, catching) = internal derangement, usually meniscal tear. History of twisting injury typical. McMurray test: flexion → rotation → extension reproduces pain/click. MRI for confirmation. Many improve with PT; surgery for failed conservative treatment.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: OA = diffuse aching, no true mechanical locking. Meniscal tear = true locking (can't fully extend), giving way, joint line tenderness. The mechanical symptoms distinguish meniscal pathology from arthritis.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if this was a young athlete with acute swelling?
whatIfScenarios.description: 18-year-old football player, knee buckled with 'pop', immediate large effusion

chiefComplaint.original: My knee has been bothering me for months
chiefComplaint.modified: 18-year-old: My knee buckled during the game. I felt a pop. It swelled up immediately and I couldn't keep playing.

feedback.correct: Pop + immediate swelling + buckling = ACL tear until proven otherwise. Lachman test is most sensitive. Hemarthrosis (blood in joint) within 2 hours is classic. MRI confirms. ACL reconstruction for active athletes wanting to return to cutting/pivoting sports.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of ACL injury pattern. What's the most sensitive exam test?
feedback.incorrect: Classic ACL tear: audible/felt 'pop,' immediate large swelling (hemarthrosis), buckling, giving way. Lachman test most sensitive. Anterior drawer less reliable acutely due to swelling. 50% have concomitant meniscal injury. MRI for operative planning.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Meniscal tear = may swell gradually over 24 hours, locking symptoms. ACL tear = pop, IMMEDIATE swelling (hemarthrosis), giving way. The immediate swelling from blood distinguishes ACL injury.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.

guidelineReferences.name: AAOS Rotator Cuff Guidelines 2019
keyPoints: Physical therapy is first-line for rotator cuff disease
NSAIDs for pain relief
Subacromial corticosteroid injection for persistent symptoms
MRI if surgery being considered or diagnosis unclear
Surgery for full-thickness tears with functional impairment
Ultrasound increasingly used for diagnosis and guided injections

male-55-rc.name: Derek Kim
male-55-rc.chiefComplaint: Right shoulder pain for 2 months, worse at night

missedConsequences.Rotator Cuff Tear: Untreated complete tear leads to fatty infiltration of muscle, making surgical repair less successful.
missedConsequences.Adhesive Capsulitis: Missed early frozen shoulder progresses to severe stiffness requiring prolonged rehabilitation.
missedConsequences.Referred Cardiac Pain: Missed cardiac etiology of shoulder pain results in delayed MI treatment.

commonPitfalls: Not examining the cervical spine - C5-6 radiculopathy refers to shoulder
Missing cardiac red flags in left shoulder pain
Failing to assess both active and passive range of motion
Over-ordering MRI for uncomplicated impingement
Not asking about overhead activities and occupational factors

mcqs.question: Shoulder pain that is WORST at night when lying on the affected side is MOST consistent with:

options.explanation: Cervical radiculopathy pain is usually worse with neck movement, not specifically lying on the shoulder.
Night pain, especially when lying on the affected shoulder, is classic for rotator cuff tendinopathy or tear due to compression of inflamed tissue.
OA pain is typically worse with activity and better with rest, not specifically worse at night.
Frozen shoulder has global ROM restriction as the defining feature, not specifically night pain.

mcqs.question: A positive 'drop arm' test (inability to slowly lower the arm from 90° abduction) suggests:

options.explanation: Impingement causes pain but not complete loss of function.
Inability to control arm lowering indicates loss of rotator cuff function, suggesting complete tear of supraspinatus.
Biceps pathology would affect elbow flexion, not abduction control.
Frozen shoulder limits motion in all directions but doesn't cause drop arm.

allDiagnoses.name: Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy/Impingement
allDiagnoses.rationale: Age 50s, lateral shoulder pain, night pain, painful arc, positive impingement signs - classic presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Rotator Cuff Tear
allDiagnoses.rationale: Same risk factors. Weakness on testing would distinguish partial from complete tear.

allDiagnoses.name: Adhesive Capsulitis (Frozen Shoulder)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must assess passive ROM. Frozen shoulder has global passive ROM restriction.

allDiagnoses.name: Cervical Radiculopathy
allDiagnoses.rationale: C5-6 can refer to shoulder. Must examine neck and check for radicular symptoms.

allDiagnoses.name: Glenohumeral Osteoarthritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would have crepitus and loss of ROM. Less common than AC joint OA.

allDiagnoses.name: AC Joint Pathology
allDiagnoses.rationale: Pain would be at top of shoulder. Cross-body adduction would be painful.

allDiagnoses.name: Biceps Tendinopathy
allDiagnoses.rationale: Pain would be anterior. Speed's test would be positive.

male-55-rc.oldcartsNarrative: "My right shoulder has been hurting for about 2 months now. It's on the outside of my shoulder, kind of deep. During the day it's maybe a 4 out of 10, but at night it really bothers me - probably a 7. I can't sleep on my right side anymore. Reaching up or out to the side makes it worse - like reaching into the back seat of my car or putting on my seatbelt. Rest and ibuprofen help some. No injury that I remember, it just started gradually."

historyQuestions.text: Do you do any overhead work or activities?
historyQuestions.answer: I paint houses for a living. Lots of overhead work with rollers and brushes. Been doing it 30 years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Occupational overhead work is primary risk factor for rotator cuff disease. Critical finding.

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any weakness in the arm?
historyQuestions.answer: Maybe a little. I have trouble lifting heavy paint cans above my head now. Used to be no problem.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Weakness suggests possible rotator cuff tear rather than just tendinopathy.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about the night pain.
historyQuestions.answer: It's the worst part. I wake up at 2-3 AM with this deep ache. Can't lie on that side at all.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Night pain is hallmark of rotator cuff pathology - inflamed tissue is compressed when lying on it.

historyQuestions.text: Any neck pain or pain running down your arm?
historyQuestions.answer: No neck pain. The pain stays in my shoulder, doesn't go down my arm.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out cervical radiculopathy as cause of shoulder pain.

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any stiffness or loss of motion?
historyQuestions.answer: It's not stiff exactly. I can move it, it just hurts. My wife says I'm guarding it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Preserved passive ROM argues against frozen shoulder. Pain-limited motion is different from true stiffness.

historyQuestions.text: Any injury or fall?
historyQuestions.answer: No fall. I don't remember any specific injury. It just came on gradually.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Degenerative tears are common without trauma. Traumatic tear would have acute onset.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had shoulder problems before?
historyQuestions.answer: Some aching over the years but nothing this bad. I always just worked through it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Chronic repetitive injury history supports cumulative damage to rotator cuff.

historyQuestions.text: Any chest pain, shortness of breath, or jaw pain?
historyQuestions.answer: No, none of that. Just the shoulder.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important to rule out cardiac etiology, especially for left shoulder pain.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just over-the-counter ibuprofen when it really hurts, and something for my blood pressure - lisinopril I think.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NSAID use important; HTN medication noted

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before any treatment recommendations

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure, controlled. Some high cholesterol. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Cardiac risk factors relevant when considering referred cardiac pain

historyQuestions.text: Any prior surgeries, especially on your shoulder?
historyQuestions.answer: Had my gallbladder out years ago. Never had any shoulder surgery.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No prior shoulder surgery - important for treatment planning

historyQuestions.text: What have you tried for the pain?
historyQuestions.answer: Ice, ibuprofen, tried to rest it but can't really with my job. My wife rubs some cream on it at night.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assessing prior conservative treatment - guides next steps

historyQuestions.text: Do you have diabetes?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my sugars have been normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Diabetes is major risk factor for adhesive capsulitis

historyQuestions.text: How is this affecting your daily life and work?
historyQuestions.answer: It's killing me. I can barely work a full day. Can't sleep. Had to turn down jobs because I can't keep up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Significant functional impairment - affects treatment urgency and decisions

occupation_current_duties.answer: House painter for 30 years. Lots of overhead work - ceilings, trim, exteriors. Use rollers, brushes, spray guns.
occupation_current_duties.relevance: Repetitive overhead work is single greatest risk factor for rotator cuff disease. 30 years of occupational exposure explains pathology.

occupation_work_volume.answer: I work 6 days a week sometimes during busy season. Can't really take time off - I'm self-employed.
occupation_work_volume.relevance: High work volume without rest days prevents healing. Self-employment creates pressure to work through pain.

occupation_equipment.answer: I use a lot of extension poles now. My guys do the ladder work. But I still do detail work overhead.
occupation_equipment.relevance: Some ergonomic modifications made but still significant overhead exposure.

recreation_activities.answer: I used to play tennis but the shoulder's been too sore. I golf when I can but even that hurts now.
recreation_activities.relevance: Recreational activities also involve shoulder stress. Loss of activities affects quality of life.

sleep_position.answer: I've always been a side sleeper, usually on my right side. Now I have to sleep on my back or left.
sleep_position.relevance: Side sleeping on affected shoulder compresses rotator cuff. Sleep modification is part of treatment.

social_handedness.answer: I'm right-handed. So of course it's my dominant arm that's the problem.
social_handedness.relevance: Dominant arm more affected due to increased use. Important for functional impact.

housing_home_tasks.answer: Even stuff at home is hard now. Reaching into high cabinets, cleaning gutters, changing light bulbs.
housing_home_tasks.relevance: Functional limitations extend beyond work. Important for assessing overall impact.

social_financial_concerns.answer: I can't afford to not work. No work, no pay. I've been pushing through but it's getting harder.
social_financial_concerns.relevance: Financial pressure drives continued overuse. May delay necessary rest or treatment.

physicalExam.finding: BP 132/78, HR 68, Temp 98.4°F. Healthy-appearing.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal vitals. No signs of systemic illness.

physicalExam.finding: Mild atrophy of supraspinatus and infraspinatus fossae on right compared to left. No swelling or deformity.
physicalExam.reasoning: Muscle atrophy suggests chronic rotator cuff dysfunction. Supports possible tear.

physicalExam.finding: Tenderness over greater tuberosity and subacromial space. AC joint non-tender. Biceps groove non-tender.
physicalExam.reasoning: Localized tenderness over supraspinatus insertion classic for rotator cuff pathology.

physicalExam.finding: Forward flexion 150° (painful arc 60-120°). Abduction 140° (painful arc 60-120°). External rotation 45°. Internal rotation reaches L3.
physicalExam.reasoning: Painful arc (60-120°) is classic impingement sign. Motion is limited by pain, not stiffness.

physicalExam.finding: Full passive ROM in all directions. Pain at end range of abduction.
physicalExam.reasoning: Full passive ROM rules out frozen shoulder. Key distinguishing finding.

physicalExam.finding: Supraspinatus (empty can test): 4/5, painful. Infraspinatus (external rotation): 4+/5. Subscapularis: 5/5. Drop arm test negative.
physicalExam.reasoning: Weakness with pain suggests partial tear or significant tendinopathy. No complete tear (drop arm negative).

physicalExam.finding: Neer test positive (pain with passive forward flexion). Hawkins test positive (pain with internal rotation at 90° flexion).
physicalExam.reasoning: Positive impingement signs confirm subacromial pathology.

physicalExam.finding: Full cervical ROM without pain. Spurling test negative bilaterally. No radicular symptoms.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal cervical exam rules out referred pain from neck.

labs.name: Labs not routinely indicated
labs.result: Clinical diagnosis - no labs needed for typical rotator cuff presentation
labs.reasoning: Rotator cuff disease is a clinical diagnosis. Labs only if inflammatory arthritis or infection suspected.

labs.name: ESR and CRP
labs.reasoning: Check only if septic arthritis or inflammatory arthritis suspected (symmetric joint involvement, prolonged morning stiffness).

imaging.name: Shoulder X-ray (AP, lateral, axillary, outlet view)
imaging.result: No fracture or dislocation. Mild subacromial spurring (Type II acromion). Mild AC joint arthrosis. Humeral head well-centered in glenoid. No superior migration of humeral head.
imaging.reasoning: X-ray rules out fracture, dislocation, calcific tendinitis, and glenohumeral OA. Outlet view shows acromion morphology. Superior humeral migration suggests chronic massive tear.

imaging.name: Shoulder ultrasound
imaging.result: Consider as alternative to MRI - operator dependent but can assess tendon integrity
imaging.reasoning: Ultrasound can identify full-thickness tears with good accuracy if experienced operator. Less expensive than MRI. Can be done dynamically.

imaging.name: MRI shoulder (without contrast)
imaging.result: Not first-line - consider if failing 6-8 weeks conservative treatment or complete tear suspected
imaging.reasoning: Gold standard for soft tissue evaluation. Shows tendon integrity, muscle atrophy, fatty infiltration. Reserve for: failed conservative treatment, acute traumatic tears, suspected complete tears, surgical planning.

procedures.name: Subacromial corticosteroid injection
procedures.result: Offered as treatment option - can be therapeutic and diagnostic
procedures.reasoning: Triamcinolone 40mg with lidocaine. If dramatic relief, confirms subacromial source. Provides 4-6 weeks relief to facilitate PT. Limit to 2-3 injections per year.

procedures.name: Subacromial lidocaine injection (diagnostic)
procedures.result: Can help differentiate impingement from other pathology
procedures.reasoning: If weakness resolves after lidocaine (pain was inhibiting effort), tendon likely intact. Persistent weakness suggests true tear.

treatments.name: Activity modification
treatments.result: Avoid overhead activities, heavy lifting, and sleeping on affected side
treatments.reasoning: Rest from aggravating activities is first-line. Avoid provocative positions. May need work modifications for painters, electricians, construction workers.

treatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, meloxicam)
treatments.result: Ibuprofen 600mg TID with food x 2 weeks, then PRN
treatments.reasoning: Anti-inflammatory treatment reduces tendon inflammation. 2-4 week course. Add PPI if GI risk factors.

treatments.name: Acetaminophen
treatments.result: Alternative if NSAIDs contraindicated
treatments.reasoning: Analgesic but not anti-inflammatory. Safer option in elderly, renal disease, or GI history.

treatments.name: Ice therapy
treatments.result: Ice 15-20 minutes several times daily
treatments.reasoning: Reduces inflammation and provides analgesia. Especially after activity.

treatments.name: Home exercise program
treatments.result: Pendulum exercises and gentle stretching started immediately
treatments.reasoning: Begin gentle ROM exercises to prevent stiffness. Pendulum exercises (Codman) are safe and maintain mobility while awaiting PT.

consults.name: Physical Therapy
consults.result: Referral placed for rotator cuff strengthening protocol - 6-8 week course
consults.reasoning: FIRST-LINE treatment for rotator cuff tendinopathy and impingement. Focuses on: rotator cuff strengthening (especially external rotators), scapular stabilization, posture correction. Evidence shows PT as effective as surgery for many patients.

consults.name: Orthopedic Surgery / Sports Medicine
consults.result: Consider if failing 3-6 months conservative treatment, acute traumatic tear, or young active patient with complete tear
consults.reasoning: Surgical indications: failed conservative management (3-6 months), complete acute tear in young active patient, progressive weakness, massive tears with functional impairment. Options: arthroscopic vs open repair, debridement, subacromial decompression.

disposition.name: Outpatient conservative management
disposition.result: Follow up in 6-8 weeks to assess PT response. If improving, continue. If not, consider MRI and injection.
disposition.reasoning: Most rotator cuff tendinopathy responds to conservative management. 6-8 weeks of PT, then reassess. Escalate if failing: injection, MRI, surgical referral.

actualDiagnosis.name: Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy with Impingement Syndrome
keyFindings: Night pain when lying on affected shoulder
Full passive ROM (argues against frozen shoulder)
teachingPoints: Painful arc (60-120°) is classic impingement sign - supraspinatus impinges under acromion
Night pain, especially when lying on affected side, is hallmark of rotator cuff pathology
Always check passive ROM - if restricted, consider frozen shoulder; if full, rotator cuff disease likely
Always examine the cervical spine - C5-6 radiculopathy mimics shoulder pain
Occupational history is critical - overhead work is primary risk factor for rotator cuff disease
Most rotator cuff tendinopathy and partial tears respond to physical therapy - surgery not first-line

guidelineReferences.name: AAOS Clinical Practice Guideline: Rotator Cuff Injuries
keyPoints: Physical therapy is first-line treatment for rotator cuff tendinopathy
NSAIDs recommended for short-term pain relief
Corticosteroid injections may provide short-term benefit but limited evidence for long-term
MRI not required before initiating conservative treatment
Surgery considered for complete tears in younger active patients or failed conservative management

guidelineReferences.name: BMJ Best Practice: Rotator Cuff Tendinopathy
keyPoints: Eccentric exercise programs have strongest evidence for tendinopathy
Subacromial decompression surgery shows limited benefit over PT in many trials
Partial thickness tears often managed conservatively
PRP injections: insufficient evidence to recommend routinely
Return to sport/work guided by pain-free function, not imaging

closureScript.diagnosis: Based on my examination, you have rotator cuff tendinopathy with impingement - basically inflammation and irritation of the tendons that control your shoulder movement. Thirty years of overhead painting has put a lot of wear on these tendons.
closureScript.plan: The main treatment is physical therapy - a specific program to strengthen the rotator cuff muscles. This takes 6-8 weeks of consistent work. I'd also recommend taking ibuprofen regularly for the next 2 weeks, using ice, and avoiding overhead work as much as possible. If PT isn't helping after a couple months, we can consider a cortisone injection or get an MRI.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Come back if you develop sudden severe weakness, complete inability to lift your arm, or if the pain becomes severe. Also return if you're not seeing improvement after 6-8 weeks of therapy.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know it's hard to limit overhead work with your job. Let's talk about what modifications might be possible. The good news is that most rotator cuff problems improve with therapy - surgery is usually not needed.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Dealing with typical life stressors. This illness is disrupting normal activities.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Has family and friends available for support. Not socially isolated.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was sudden weakness after a fall?
whatIfScenarios.description: Fell on outstretched arm, now cannot lift arm against gravity

chiefComplaint.original: My shoulder has been bothering me for a couple months
chiefComplaint.modified: I fell yesterday and now I can't lift my arm at all - it just won't work

feedback.correct: Acute trauma + sudden inability to lift arm = acute rotator cuff tear until proven otherwise. Get X-ray (rule out fracture), then MRI urgently. Acute complete tears in active patients often need surgical repair within 4-6 weeks for best outcomes.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of acute tear. What's the urgency for surgical evaluation?
feedback.incorrect: Acute complete rotator cuff tears: trauma + sudden weakness + can't lift arm. Different from chronic degenerative tears. MRI confirms. Active patients with acute complete tears benefit from EARLY repair (within 4-6 weeks) - muscle retraction and atrophy occur over time.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Chronic tendinopathy = gradual, painful, but can still lift arm. Acute complete tear = sudden, weakness > pain, cannot actively lift arm. Acute tears may need urgent surgery; chronic tears often managed conservatively.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if passive ROM was severely limited?
whatIfScenarios.description: Gradual stiffening of shoulder, diabetic patient, cannot raise arm even when helped

chiefComplaint.original: My shoulder has been bothering me for a couple months
chiefComplaint.modified: My shoulder has been getting stiffer for months. Now I can barely move it even with help.

feedback.correct: Limited PASSIVE ROM = capsular problem, not rotator cuff. Frozen shoulder has global restriction in all planes (especially external rotation). Common in diabetics, hypothyroid, post-injury. Self-limiting but can take 1-3 years. PT + possible injection.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of frozen shoulder. What population is at high risk?
feedback.incorrect: Frozen shoulder (adhesive capsulitis): restricted PASSIVE ROM in all directions, especially external rotation. Distinct from rotator cuff disease (full passive ROM). Risk factors: diabetes (5x risk), hypothyroidism, post-immobilization. Usually self-limiting over 1-3 years. PT, injections, rarely manipulation under anesthesia.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Rotator cuff disease = full passive ROM, active motion limited by pain/weakness. Frozen shoulder = restricted passive AND active ROM. Examining passive ROM distinguishes these.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if there was neck pain and arm numbness?
whatIfScenarios.description: Shoulder pain with neck stiffness, numbness radiating to thumb, and weak biceps

chiefComplaint.original: My shoulder has been bothering me for a couple months
chiefComplaint.modified: My shoulder hurts but my neck is also stiff, and I have numbness going down to my thumb

feedback.correct: Shoulder pain + neck symptoms + dermatomal radiation = cervical radiculopathy until proven otherwise. C5-C6 commonly refers to shoulder. Spurling test (extension + rotation + compression) reproduces radicular symptoms. MRI cervical spine, not shoulder.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of cervical origin. How do you distinguish from true shoulder pathology?
feedback.incorrect: Always examine the cervical spine with shoulder pain. C5-C6 radiculopathy commonly mimics shoulder pain. Clues: neck pain/stiffness, dermatomal numbness (C6 = thumb, C7 = middle finger), weakness in myotome, positive Spurling test. Image the cervical spine, not just shoulder.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: True shoulder pathology = localized to shoulder, negative cervical exam, Spurling negative. Cervical radiculopathy = neck involvement, dermatomal numbness, Spurling positive. Examining the neck is essential in all shoulder evaluations.

female-35-strain.name: Linda Chen
female-35-strain.chiefComplaint: My lower back is killing me

missedConsequences.Cauda Equina Syndrome: Always must screen for this surgical emergency.

commonPitfalls: Over-imaging acute mechanical back pain
Prescribing opioids for uncomplicated back strain
Not addressing psychosocial factors that predict chronicity

allDiagnoses.name: Mechanical Low Back Pain/Strain
allDiagnoses.rationale: Activity-related onset, no red flags, no radicular symptoms, reproducible muscle tenderness.

allDiagnoses.name: Disc Herniation
allDiagnoses.rationale: No radicular symptoms, negative SLR.

allDiagnoses.name: Cauda Equina
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must screen but no red flags present.

allDiagnoses.name: Vertebral Fracture
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would need trauma history or osteoporosis.

female-35-strain.oldcartsNarrative: "Started 3 days ago after I spent the weekend doing yard work - lots of bending and lifting. Woke up Monday barely able to move. The pain is right in my lower back, both sides. No pain going down my legs. It's stiff in the morning and hurts when I bend or twist."

historyQuestions.text: What were you doing when it started?
historyQuestions.answer: Weekend yard work - raking, lifting bags of mulch, bending over planting flowers for hours.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Clear mechanical trigger with repetitive bending and lifting.

historyQuestions.text: Does the pain go anywhere else?
historyQuestions.answer: No, just my lower back. Doesn't go down my legs.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absence of radiation argues against radiculopathy.

historyQuestions.text: Any numbness, weakness, or bladder/bowel problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, none of those.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Must always screen for cauda equina red flags.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Birth control pills. I've been taking ibuprofen (Advil) around the clock for this.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NSAID use noted; OCP for context

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before prescribing

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm healthy. First time with back pain like this.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No chronic conditions or prior back problems

historyQuestions.text: Any fevers, chills, or unexplained weight loss?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Screening for infection or malignancy red flags

historyQuestions.text: Any history of cancer?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important red flag - cancer can metastasize to spine

historyQuestions.text: What makes it worse or better?
historyQuestions.answer: Bending, twisting, getting up from sitting. Rest and ibuprofen help. Worst in the morning when I'm stiff.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Mechanical pattern - worse with activity, better with rest

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

occupation_work_type.answer: Office job. Sit at a desk 8 hours a day.
occupation_work_type.relevance: Sedentary work leads to deconditioning; sudden activity causes injury.

social_exercise.answer: Don't exercise regularly. This was way more than I usually do.
social_exercise.relevance: Sudden increase in activity without conditioning is classic strain trigger.

occupation_ergonomics.answer: My office chair is terrible. I slouch a lot.
occupation_ergonomics.relevance: Poor posture contributes to back vulnerability.

physicalExam.finding: Normal alignment. Paraspinal muscle spasm visible on right. No deformity.
physicalExam.reasoning: Muscle spasm consistent with strain.

physicalExam.finding: Bilateral paraspinal tenderness L3-S1. No midline bony tenderness. No step-off.
physicalExam.reasoning: Muscle tenderness without bony tenderness = strain not fracture.

physicalExam.finding: Flexion limited by pain. Extension limited. Lateral bending limited bilaterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: Diffuse limitation consistent with muscle guarding.

physicalExam.finding: Motor 5/5 throughout. Sensation intact. DTRs 2+ symmetric. SLR negative bilaterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal neuro exam rules out radiculopathy.

labs.name: No laboratory testing indicated for typical mechanical back pain
labs.result: Labs are not indicated for acute mechanical low back pain without red flag symptoms
labs.reasoning: Acute low back pain is a CLINICAL diagnosis. Labs do not confirm or change management of typical mechanical back pain. ACP/APS guidelines explicitly recommend AGAINST routine labs.

labs.name: CBC, ESR, CRP
labs.result: Would only check if RED FLAGS present: fever, IVDU, immunocompromised, unexplained weight loss, or concern for infection/malignancy
labs.reasoning: Reserve for: suspected spinal infection (fever, IVDU), suspected malignancy (cancer history, unexplained weight loss >10 lbs), inflammatory arthritis (prolonged morning stiffness >1 hour).

imaging.name: No imaging indicated for acute mechanical back pain
imaging.result: Imaging NOT indicated in first 4-6 weeks for typical mechanical low back pain without red flags. Studies show early imaging does NOT improve outcomes and leads to unnecessary intervention.
imaging.reasoning: ACP/APS GUIDELINES: Do NOT obtain imaging in first 6 weeks unless red flags present. Imaging findings (disc bulges, degeneration) are common in asymptomatic people and often unrelated to pain. Early imaging leads to unnecessary surgery and worse outcomes.

imaging.name: Lumbar spine X-ray
imaging.result: Not indicated for typical presentation
imaging.reasoning: X-ray only indicated for: trauma with fracture concern, osteoporosis risk, suspected spondylolisthesis, cancer history. X-ray does NOT show disc disease, muscles, or nerves.

imaging.name: MRI lumbar spine
imaging.result: NOT indicated for typical mechanical back pain. Would consider if: progressive neurological deficit, cauda equina syndrome, failure to improve after 6 weeks of conservative treatment, or red flags.
imaging.reasoning: MRI is sensitive but NOT specific - finds 'abnormalities' in 30-40% of asymptomatic young adults. Only order with clear indication. Does NOT improve outcomes when ordered routinely.

treatments.name: Activity modification - STAY ACTIVE
treatments.result: Continue normal activities as tolerated. Avoid bed rest. Staying active leads to faster recovery than bed rest.
treatments.reasoning: BED REST IS HARMFUL and prolongs recovery. Evidence shows staying active leads to faster recovery and less chronic pain. Continue normal activities with reasonable modification.

treatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen)
treatments.result: Naproxen 500mg BID or Ibuprofen 600mg TID with food for 1-2 weeks. First-line pharmacotherapy.
treatments.reasoning: First-line treatment for acute low back pain. Anti-inflammatory and analgesic. Use scheduled dosing (not PRN) for first 1-2 weeks. GI precautions as appropriate.

treatments.name: Acetaminophen
treatments.result: Can add to NSAIDs for additional analgesia. Max 3g/day. Evidence for efficacy as monotherapy is limited.
treatments.reasoning: Can supplement NSAIDs. However, recent evidence (PACE trial) suggests acetaminophen alone is NOT more effective than placebo for acute low back pain.

treatments.name: Skeletal muscle relaxant (cyclobenzaprine)
treatments.result: Cyclobenzaprine 10mg at bedtime PRN for 1-2 weeks if significant muscle spasm. Sedating - use at night.
treatments.reasoning: May help if significant muscle spasm component. Sedating - use primarily at night. Limit duration to 2 weeks to avoid dependence. Avoid in elderly.

treatments.name: Ice and/or heat therapy
treatments.result: Ice or heat to affected area 15-20 minutes several times daily. Patient preference acceptable.
treatments.reasoning: Superficial heat has modest evidence for short-term relief. Ice may help acute inflammation. Patient preference is reasonable - use whichever feels better.

treatments.name: Avoid opioids
treatments.result: Opioids NOT recommended for acute low back pain per guidelines. NSAIDs more effective and safer.
treatments.reasoning: Multiple guidelines recommend AGAINST opioids for acute low back pain. NSAIDs are more effective. Opioids associated with prolonged disability, hyperalgesia, and addiction. Avoid or use only briefly if absolutely necessary.

treatments.name: Systemic steroids not indicated
treatments.result: Oral steroids are NOT effective for mechanical low back pain without radiculopathy
treatments.reasoning: Studies show oral steroids do NOT improve outcomes in acute low back pain without radiculopathy. Side effects without benefit.

consults.name: Physical therapy
consults.result: Consider PT referral if not improving in 2-4 weeks. Core strengthening, stretching, body mechanics education.
consults.reasoning: PT is evidence-based for subacute/chronic low back pain. Core stabilization, McKenzie exercises, education on body mechanics. Can start early if motivated patient.

consults.name: Spine surgery
consults.result: NOT indicated for mechanical back pain without radiculopathy or structural pathology
consults.reasoning: Surgery not indicated for non-specific low back pain. Only consider for: progressive neurological deficit, cauda equina, structural abnormality with correlating symptoms.

disposition.name: Outpatient management with follow-up
disposition.result: Follow up in 2-4 weeks if not improving. Most patients improve within 4-6 weeks. Clear return precautions for red flag symptoms.
disposition.reasoning: 90% of acute low back pain resolves within 6 weeks. Outpatient management appropriate. Return if: neurological symptoms, bladder/bowel dysfunction, fever, weight loss, worsening despite treatment.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Mechanical Low Back Pain (Muscle Strain)
keyFindings: Paraspinal muscle tenderness without midline bony tenderness
No red flag symptoms (no cauda equina, fever, weight loss)
teachingPoints: Most acute low back pain is mechanical and self-limited (90% resolve in 6 weeks)
Imaging NOT indicated for uncomplicated back pain without red flags
Red flags: cauda equina symptoms, fever, weight loss, trauma, cancer history, IV drug use
Best treatment: stay active, NSAIDs, PT. Bedrest is harmful.
Psychosocial yellow flags predict chronicity: catastrophizing, fear-avoidance, depression

closureScript.diagnosis: You have a muscle strain in your lower back from the yard work. Your nerves are not affected.
closureScript.plan: This will get better on its own over the next few weeks. Stay as active as you can - don't lie in bed. Ibuprofen or naproxen for pain and inflammation. Ice or heat, whichever feels better. Gentle stretching.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Come back if you develop numbness, weakness, or any bladder/bowel problems - those are rare but important.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: You don't need X-rays or MRI right now. Studies show they don't help and often find things that aren't actually causing your pain. If you're not better in 4-6 weeks, we can reassess.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had a history of cancer?
whatIfScenarios.description: Back pain in patient with history of breast or prostate cancer

chiefComplaint.original: My back has been hurting since I did yard work
chiefComplaint.modified: My back has been hurting for 2 weeks. It's worse at night. I had breast cancer 5 years ago.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Metastatic spinal disease until proven otherwise

feedback.correct: Cancer history + back pain = spinal metastases until proven otherwise. Red flag! MRI spine is indicated. Breast, prostate, lung, kidney, thyroid commonly metastasize to spine. Night pain is especially concerning.
feedback.partial: Good concern for metastases. What imaging is needed?
feedback.incorrect: Cancer history is a major red flag in back pain. Breast, prostate, lung, kidney, thyroid commonly metastasize to spine. Night pain, not relieved by rest, progressive = concerning. MRI is imaging of choice (not X-ray - misses early mets).

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: No cancer history + mechanical features = observation reasonable. Cancer history + back pain = image it. The history changes everything.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the pain had persisted for 6 months?
whatIfScenarios.description: Back pain for 6 months, multiple doctor visits, not improving

chiefComplaint.original: My back has been hurting since I did yard work
chiefComplaint.modified: My back has hurt for 6 months. I've tried everything. I can't work. I'm frustrated and depressed.

expectedReasoning.topDiagnosis: Chronic low back pain with psychosocial factors

feedback.correct: Chronic low back pain (>12 weeks) requires different approach than acute. Address psychosocial factors: depression, fear-avoidance, catastrophizing. Multidisciplinary treatment: PT, cognitive behavioral therapy, possibly pain psychology. Opioids are harmful.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of chronic pain complexity. What factors perpetuate it?
feedback.incorrect: Chronic back pain transitions from tissue injury to central sensitization and psychosocial factors. Yellow flags: depression, catastrophizing, fear-avoidance, compensation issues. Treatment: multidisciplinary (PT, psychology, pain management), NOT more opioids. Address beliefs about pain.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Acute back pain = self-limited, conservative treatment. Chronic back pain = biopsychosocial model, multidisciplinary treatment, address yellow flags. Duration and psychosocial factors change the approach.

female-28-costo.name: Anthony Ramirez
female-28-costo.chiefComplaint: Sharp chest pain that hurts when I breathe

missedConsequences.Pulmonary Embolism: Can be pleuritic. Must consider in right clinical context.

commonPitfalls: Dismissing chest pain without proper evaluation
Not reproducing the pain on exam to confirm MSK cause
Missing PE which can also cause pleuritic pain

allDiagnoses.name: Costochondritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Young woman, reproducible sternal tenderness, no cardiac risk factors, normal vital signs.

allDiagnoses.name: Pulmonary Embolism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Pleuritic pain warrants consideration. Low probability here but assess risk factors.

allDiagnoses.name: Pericarditis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect positional variation, friction rub, ECG changes.

allDiagnoses.name: ACS
allDiagnoses.rationale: Very low probability in 28-year-old female, reproducible MSK pain.

female-28-costo.oldcartsNarrative: "Started about a week ago after I helped my friend move apartments - lots of heavy lifting. It's a sharp pain right here on my breastbone. Worse when I take a deep breath, cough, or press on it. Doesn't go anywhere else. No shortness of breath, no palpitations."

historyQuestions.text: Does pressing on your chest make it worse?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! That's exactly where it hurts when you push there.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Reproducible tenderness on palpation strongly suggests MSK cause.

historyQuestions.text: Does it hurt more with deep breaths?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, taking a deep breath or coughing makes it sharper.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pleuritic quality - must consider multiple causes.

historyQuestions.text: Any recent travel, surgery, or birth control pills?
historyQuestions.answer: No travel, no surgery. I'm on birth control but have been for years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assessing VTE risk factors. OCP is one risk factor but low overall risk.

historyQuestions.text: Does it come on with exercise or exertion?
historyQuestions.answer: No, not really. It's more when I move certain ways or take a deep breath. Not like during exercise.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Non-exertional pattern argues against angina

historyQuestions.text: When did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: About a week ago after I helped my friend move. Lots of heavy lifting.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Clear mechanical trigger with heavy lifting

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Just birth control pills. I've been taking ibuprofen for this which helps a little.
historyQuestions.reasoning: OCP noted; NSAIDs providing relief supports inflammatory/MSK cause

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before prescribing

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm healthy. Never had any heart problems.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No cardiac history in young patient

historyQuestions.text: Any leg pain or swelling?
historyQuestions.answer: No, legs are fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No DVT symptoms - lowers PE probability

historyQuestions.text: Any shortness of breath?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I can breathe fine when I'm not taking deep breaths. Just hurts.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No dyspnea at rest - reassuring

recreation_activity.answer: Helped friend move last weekend. Carried boxes, furniture. More lifting than I usually do.
recreation_activity.relevance: Clear mechanical trigger for costochondral strain.

occupation_work_type.answer: Office job. Sit at computer all day.
occupation_work_type.relevance: Sudden increased activity in sedentary person common costochondritis trigger.

physicalExam.finding: Marked tenderness to palpation at left 3rd-5th costochondral junctions. Reproduces her pain exactly. No swelling.
physicalExam.reasoning: REPRODUCTION of pain on palpation is key diagnostic finding.

physicalExam.finding: S1, S2 regular. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
physicalExam.reasoning: No pericardial friction rub.

physicalExam.finding: Clear bilaterally. No rales, wheezes, or rubs.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal lung exam. No pleural rub.

labs.name: Labs not routinely indicated
labs.result: No laboratory testing needed for classic costochondritis with reproducible tenderness
labs.reasoning: Costochondritis is a clinical diagnosis based on history and reproducible palpation tenderness. Labs do not confirm or exclude.

labs.name: Troponin
labs.reasoning: Only if concern for ACS based on history/risk factors. This patient has low pre-test probability - young, reproducible pain, no cardiac risk factors.

labs.name: D-dimer
labs.reasoning: Low pre-test probability for PE (no immobilization, no prior VTE, no malignancy). OCP alone doesn't warrant D-dimer for typical costochondritis. Wells score 0.

imaging.name: ECG
imaging.result: Normal sinus rhythm at 72 bpm. No ST changes. No PR depression. No TWI. Normal QTc.
imaging.reasoning: May be reasonable for patient reassurance. Normal ECG confirms no pericarditis (PR depression) or ischemia. Low yield in classic presentation but often done.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Not routinely indicated for classic costochondritis
imaging.reasoning: Costochondritis doesn't show on X-ray. Only obtain if concern for pneumonia, rib fracture, or other pathology based on history.

imaging.name: CT Angiography for PE
imaging.result: NOT INDICATED - low probability
imaging.reasoning: Wells score 0 (no clinical features of PE). Reproducible musculoskeletal pain. Young patient on OCP alone does not warrant CT. Would need additional risk factors or symptoms.

treatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen)
treatments.result: Naproxen 500mg BID with food for 1-2 weeks. Already taking ibuprofen with some relief - confirms inflammatory nature.
treatments.reasoning: First-line treatment. Scheduled dosing (not PRN) more effective. 1-2 weeks course. GI protection if prolonged use.

treatments.name: Topical NSAID (diclofenac gel)
treatments.result: Diclofenac gel to affected area TID as adjunct
treatments.reasoning: Can supplement oral NSAIDs for local anti-inflammatory effect. Less systemic absorption.

treatments.name: Acetaminophen
treatments.result: Can add for pain relief. Less effective than NSAIDs for inflammation.
treatments.reasoning: Safe adjunct for analgesia. Less effective alone since this is inflammatory.

treatments.name: Ice and/or heat application
treatments.result: Ice 15-20 min several times daily, or heat if more comfortable
treatments.reasoning: Ice for acute inflammation. Heat for comfort. Patient preference acceptable.

treatments.name: Activity modification
treatments.result: Avoid heavy lifting and repetitive arm movements until resolved
treatments.reasoning: Avoid activities that strain chest wall. Temporary modification until pain resolved.

treatments.name: Local corticosteroid injection
treatments.result: Consider if refractory to NSAIDs after 2-4 weeks
treatments.reasoning: Lidocaine with steroid injection to costochondral junction can provide relief in refractory cases. Usually not first-line.

consults.name: No specialty consultation needed
consults.result: Primary care can manage typical costochondritis
consults.reasoning: Costochondritis is managed in primary care. No referral needed for typical cases.

consults.name: Rheumatology
consults.result: Only if recurrent, atypical features, or concern for inflammatory arthritis
consults.reasoning: Refer only if: recurrent episodes, associated joint symptoms, concern for seronegative spondyloarthropathy (SAPHO syndrome, psoriatic arthritis).

disposition.name: Discharge with conservative treatment
disposition.result: Discharge with NSAIDs, activity modification, and reassurance. Follow up PRN if not improving in 2-4 weeks.
disposition.reasoning: Costochondritis is self-limited. Most resolve in weeks to months. Clear return precautions for concerning symptoms.

actualDiagnosis.name: Costochondritis
keyFindings: Pain worsens with breathing and movement
Low risk for PE/ACS based on history
teachingPoints: Costochondritis = REPRODUCIBLE tenderness at costochondral junctions
If you can reproduce the exact pain on palpation, cardiac cause is very unlikely
Still must consider dangerous causes - PE can be pleuritic, pericarditis can be sharp
Treatment is NSAIDs, rest, and reassurance. Resolves in weeks to months.

closureScript.diagnosis: You have costochondritis - inflammation where your ribs connect to your breastbone. It's from the strain of all that lifting when you helped your friend move.
closureScript.plan: This is painful but not dangerous. Take ibuprofen or naproxen regularly for the next 1-2 weeks. Avoid heavy lifting until it resolves. It can take several weeks to fully go away.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Come back if you develop shortness of breath, pain spreading to your arm or jaw, fever, or if the pain changes character.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I understand chest pain is scary. The fact that I can reproduce your exact pain by pressing means this is coming from your chest wall, not your heart.

Drank well water all my life, also lots of brick tea. Many people in my village have same problem - they say it's the water."

historyQuestions.text: Others affected?
historyQuestions.answer: Very common in my village. Young people can't bend their spines. Government knows about the problem.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Endemic pattern.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

diet_fluoride.answer: High-fluoride well water + brick tea (additional fluoride source)

female-82-fall.chiefComplaint: Hip pain after a fall, can't walk
female-82-fall.variantDescription: Hip fracture in elderly - surgical emergency

missedConsequences.Delirium: Common post-fracture, associated with poor outcomes if not recognized.

commonPitfalls: Missing hip fracture on X-ray (get MRI if high suspicion with negative X-ray)
Delaying surgery beyond 48 hours
Not optimizing medically before surgery
Forgetting DVT prophylaxis

mcqs.question: An elderly patient with hip pain after fall and SHORTENED, EXTERNALLY ROTATED leg MOST likely has:

options.explanation: Wouldn't cause limb shortening and rotation.
Classic presentation: shortened, externally rotated leg due to unopposed muscle pull on proximal fragment.
Posterior dislocation causes shortened, INTERNALLY rotated leg.
Wouldn't cause isolated leg position change.

mcqs.question: The target time to surgery for hip fracture is:

options.explanation: Not always possible or necessary.
Surgery within 24-48h reduces mortality, delirium, pressure ulcers, and length of stay.
Too long - increases complications.
Hip fracture is urgent - not elective.

allDiagnoses.name: Femoral Neck Fracture
allDiagnoses.rationale: Mechanism, age, classic exam findings.

allDiagnoses.name: Intertrochanteric Fracture
allDiagnoses.rationale: Other common hip fracture pattern.

allDiagnoses.name: Pathologic Fracture
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must consider underlying malignancy in elderly.

allDiagnoses.name: Hip Contusion/No Fracture
allDiagnoses.rationale: Leg positioning strongly suggests fracture.

female-82-fall.oldcartsNarrative: "I fell at home this morning. I was getting up from my chair and my leg just gave out. I landed on my right side. I couldn't get up - the pain in my hip was too bad. I was on the floor for about an hour before my daughter found me. I can't put any weight on it. I take medication for my bones - osteoporosis. I had a hip replaced on the left side 5 years ago."

historyQuestions.text: How did you fall?
historyQuestions.answer: I was getting up from my chair and my leg just gave out. I fell onto my right side.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Low-energy mechanism (fall from standing) in elderly with osteoporosis = high fracture risk.

historyQuestions.text: Can you put any weight on it?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I can't stand at all. Too painful.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Inability to bear weight strongly suggests fracture.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have osteoporosis?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I take alendronate for it. I've had it for years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Osteoporosis dramatically increases fracture risk with minimal trauma.

historyQuestions.text: Any prior hip surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: I had my left hip replaced 5 years ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior hip surgery indicates bone disease. Also important for surgical planning.

historyQuestions.text: Do you take blood thinners?
historyQuestions.answer: No blood thinners. Just alendronate, lisinopril, and a baby aspirin.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Anticoagulation affects surgical timing - may need to hold or reverse.

historyQuestions.text: How were you functioning before the fall? Walking independently?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I live alone. I walk with a cane but I'm independent. I still drive.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Baseline function is critical for surgical decision-making and goals of care.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

physicalExam.finding: BP 142/78, HR 92, RR 18, SpO2 96%. Mildly tachycardic, likely pain-related.
physicalExam.reasoning: Vital signs stable. Pain causing mild tachycardia.

physicalExam.finding: Elderly woman lying in bed, in obvious discomfort. Grimaces with any movement of right leg.
physicalExam.reasoning: Pain with leg movement is classic.

physicalExam.finding: Right leg is SHORTENED and EXTERNALLY ROTATED compared to left. Leg held in slight flexion.
physicalExam.reasoning: CLASSIC: Shortened + externally rotated = hip fracture. Muscles pull proximal fragment into this position.

physicalExam.finding: Refuses any movement - too painful. Any passive motion elicits severe pain. Unable to do log roll.
physicalExam.reasoning: Severe pain with any motion - don't force the exam, you'll hurt her.

physicalExam.finding: Right foot: Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses palpable. Sensation intact. Wiggles toes. Cap refill <2 seconds.
physicalExam.reasoning: CRITICAL: Must document distal neurovascular status. This is intact - good sign.

physicalExam.finding: No open wounds. Bruising over right hip. No skin breakdown (was on floor 1 hour).
physicalExam.reasoning: Check for open fracture (would change urgency) and pressure injuries from time on floor.

imaging.name: X-ray Right Hip (AP and Lateral)
imaging.result: Displaced subcapital femoral neck fracture. Garden classification IV (completely displaced). Femoral head displaced from acetabulum.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms fracture and classification. Garden IV = completely displaced, blood supply disrupted, high risk of avascular necrosis. Determines surgical approach.

imaging.name: X-ray Pelvis AP
imaging.result: Confirms right femoral neck fracture. No associated pelvic fracture. Left total hip replacement in place, well-positioned.
imaging.reasoning: Always get pelvis view in hip trauma - may have associated pelvic injury or contralateral hip pathology.

imaging.name: MRI Hip (if X-ray negative but high suspicion)
imaging.result: Not needed - fracture visible on X-ray.
imaging.reasoning: MRI for occult hip fracture when X-ray negative but high clinical suspicion. 10-15% of hip fractures are X-ray occult.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray (pre-op)
imaging.result: Mild cardiomegaly. No acute cardiopulmonary process.
imaging.reasoning: Pre-operative assessment. Rule out CHF, pneumonia. Baseline for elderly patient.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Pre-op baseline. Hip fractures can have 500-1500mL blood loss. Type and screen if anemic.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Pre-op baseline. Assess renal function for medication dosing and contrast if needed.

labs.name: PT/INR/PTT
labs.reasoning: Pre-op coagulation assessment. Critical if on anticoagulants.

labs.name: Type and Screen
labs.result: Blood type O positive. Antibody screen negative.
labs.reasoning: Blood products may be needed for surgery. Always type and screen hip fractures.

labs.name: Urinalysis
labs.reasoning: Rule out UTI which could delay surgery or cause post-op complications.

labs.name: Troponin (if cardiac symptoms)
labs.reasoning: Consider in elderly with cardiac history or unexplained tachycardia. Demand ischemia can occur from pain/stress.

procedures.name: 12-lead ECG
procedures.result: Normal sinus rhythm at 88 bpm. No ST changes. Old Q waves in III (prior inferior MI per history). No acute changes.
procedures.reasoning: Pre-op cardiac risk assessment. Identify arrhythmias, ischemia, or conduction abnormalities.

procedures.name: Foley catheter
procedures.result: Foley placed for accurate I/O monitoring and patient comfort pre-op.
procedures.reasoning: Often placed for OR. Allows monitoring of urine output and prevents retention.

procedures.name: Fascia iliaca nerve block
procedures.result: Fascia iliaca block placed by anesthesia for pain control. Excellent pain relief achieved.
procedures.reasoning: Regional anesthesia for pain control. Reduces opioid requirements and delirium risk. Can be done in ED.

treatments.name: Pain management
treatments.result: Fascia iliaca block for primary analgesia. Acetaminophen 1g IV scheduled. Low-dose opioids PRN (morphine 2mg IV q4h PRN).
treatments.reasoning: Multimodal pain control. Nerve blocks are ideal. Minimize opioids (cause delirium). Scheduled acetaminophen is helpful.

treatments.name: DVT prophylaxis
treatments.result: Mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs on unaffected leg). Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily started (hold 12h pre-op).
treatments.reasoning: High DVT/PE risk. Start prophylaxis immediately. LMWH or mechanical. Balance bleeding risk with thrombosis risk pre-op.

treatments.name: Delirium prevention
treatments.result: Reorientation, glasses/hearing aids in place, sleep protocol, avoid benzos, minimize anticholinergics.
treatments.reasoning: Hip fracture patients have 35-65% delirium rate. Non-pharmacologic prevention is key. Avoid deliriogenic meds.

treatments.name: IV fluids
treatments.result: Maintenance fluids started. NPO for surgery.
treatments.reasoning: Maintain hydration. Elderly often dehydrated from time on floor.

treatments.name: Osteoporosis treatment (post-op)
treatments.result: Continue alendronate. Will reassess calcium/vitamin D. Consider anabolic therapy post-op.
treatments.reasoning: Osteoporosis treatment reduces future fracture risk. Bisphosphonates, calcium, vitamin D. Consider teriparatide for high-risk patients.

consults.name: Orthopedic Surgery (URGENT)
consults.result: Ortho consulted emergently. Plan for hemiarthroplasty given displaced femoral neck fracture in elderly patient. Goal surgery within 24-48 hours.
consults.reasoning: URGENT surgical consultation. Displaced femoral neck fractures in elderly typically require arthroplasty (hemiarthroplasty or total hip). Surgery within 24-48h improves outcomes.

consults.name: Medicine/Geriatrics co-management
consults.result: Medicine consult for perioperative optimization. Will manage medical comorbidities, delirium prevention, and post-op care.
consults.reasoning: Geriatric/medicine co-management improves outcomes in hip fracture patients. Reduces complications and mortality.

consults.name: Anesthesiology (pre-op)
consults.result: Anesthesia notified for surgical planning. Will evaluate for regional vs general anesthesia.
consults.reasoning: Pre-op anesthesia evaluation. Spinal/regional anesthesia may be preferred in elderly (reduces delirium, cardiac stress).

consults.name: Physical Therapy
consults.result: PT consulted for post-op rehabilitation planning. Goal is early mobilization day 1 post-op.
consults.reasoning: Early mobilization is critical. PT starts post-op day 1. Reduces complications and improves functional outcomes.

disposition.name: Admit to ortho/medicine service
disposition.result: Admitted to orthopedic surgery service with medicine co-management. Goal surgery within 24 hours.
disposition.reasoning: All hip fractures require admission for surgical repair. Delay beyond 48 hours increases mortality.

disposition.name: Surgical timing
disposition.result: Goal surgery within 24 hours. No medical contraindications identified. Pre-op clearance obtained.
disposition.reasoning: Surgery within 24-48 hours is goal. Delay beyond 48h increases mortality, pressure ulcers, and complications.

disposition.name: Goals of care discussion
disposition.result: Goals of care discussed with patient and family. Patient wants surgery and rehabilitation. HCP identified.
disposition.reasoning: Important in elderly with serious injury. Understand patient's goals. Document healthcare proxy.

disposition.name: Post-operative plan
disposition.result: Post-op: DVT prophylaxis x 4 weeks, early mobilization, PT/OT, delirium monitoring, pain management, discharge planning (likely rehab).
disposition.reasoning: Anticipate post-op needs. Most elderly hip fracture patients go to rehab before home.

disposition.name: Family communication
disposition.result: Daughter updated on plan. Hip fracture is serious - discussed mortality risk and rehabilitation expectations.
disposition.reasoning: Family communication is critical. Set realistic expectations. Hip fracture has significant morbidity/mortality.

actualDiagnosis.name: Displaced Right Femoral Neck Fracture (Garden IV)
keyFindings: X-ray confirms displaced femoral neck fracture
teachingPoints: Classic exam: Shortened, externally rotated leg = hip fracture until proven otherwise
Surgery within 24-48 hours improves outcomes
Femoral neck fractures have blood supply concerns - AVN risk
Treatment depends on displacement: Non-displaced → screws; Displaced → hemiarthroplasty or total hip (in active elderly)
Pre-op optimization: DVT prophylaxis, pain control, avoid delirium, medical clearance
Post-op: Early mobilization, PT, DVT prophylaxis, osteoporosis treatment
Hip fractures in elderly have ~20-30% one-year mortality

female-82-fall.teachingPoint: This is one of the most common orthopedic emergencies. Key points: 1) Recognize the classic position (shortened, externally rotated), 2) DOCUMENT distal neurovascular status, 3) Surgery within 24-48h is the goal, 4) Pre-op optimization matters but shouldn't delay surgery excessively, 5) These patients are FRAGILE - watch for delirium, pressure ulcers, DVT, and functional decline. The orthopedic surgery team and medicine/geriatrics co-management improves outcomes.
female-82-fall.clinicalPearl: Hip fracture is often called 'the beginning of the end' for elderly patients - 30-day mortality is 5-10%, one-year mortality is 20-30%. BUT good perioperative care makes a huge difference. Key interventions: Early surgery, DVT prophylaxis (LMWH or mechanical), delirium prevention (avoid benzos, treat pain, mobilize early), early PT/mobilization, and treat underlying osteoporosis.

questions.stem: Based on the clinical presentation, which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.text: Systematic history and focused physical examination
options.explanation: A targeted history and physical exam is the essential first step in clinical reasoning, guiding hypothesis generation and differential diagnosis.
Empiric treatment before adequate evaluation risks missing the correct diagnosis and may cause harm.
Diagnostic testing should be hypothesis-driven, not shotgun; targeted tests based on clinical reasoning yield better outcomes.
Consultation may be appropriate later but should follow initial clinical assessment and diagnostic reasoning.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.
